Cloud storage comparison

Each cloud provider offers storage services that leverage NetApp ONTAP.

AWS

- Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP: Provides functionality equivalent to an on-premise NetApp environment

- SnapMirror for direct volume replication

- multi-protocol support (NFS/SMB)

Azure

- Azure NetApp Files (ANF): NetApp services fully integrated into Azure Ecosystem

- Creates ANF volumes directly from the Azure Portal

- Data Migration with NetApp tools or NFS mount copy

Google Cloud

- Google Cloud NetApp Volumes: NetApp services fully integrated into Google Cloud environment

- Use as SnapMirror target via Cloud Volumes ONTAP

Selection Considerations

The choice of storage is determined by performance needs and operational requirements.

Storage selection considerations

- Make it simple: Native cloud storage (EBS/Managed Disks etc.)

- Focusing on performance, and snapshot/clone capabilities: NetApp based solution

- Backup/DR planning: Repurpose SnapMirror used for the migration during DR replication or utilize cloud snapshots or Backint

Summary

Your method will depend on the type of database you want to migrate, the environment you are migrating to, your downtime requirement, etc. Choosing the right migration tool/technique combination and investing enough time in planning and preparing for the migration can make migrating your database a smooth and secure process.

Migrating Database (SAP HANA and non-HANA DB)

Database migration is one of the key elements when migrating to S/4HANA. The on-premise version of S/4HANA comes on top of a common database platform, the SAP HANA DB, so if ECC 6.0 is currently on a database other than HANA (e.g., Oracle, DB2, SQL Server, MaxDB, etc.), then a database platform migration to HANA is required. This has significant implications for your migration plan.

Scenario

- ECC on non-HANA -> S/4HANA on HANA: Typical brownfield conversion scenario. It needs migration from the current database into SAP HANA. Using SAP's SUM (Software Update Manager) with DMO, this can be achieved in a single step. Alternatively, a two-step approach would be to first migrate the ECC database to HANA (Suite on HANA configuration, still an ECC application) and then upgrade the S/4HANA application. One-step migration is preferred whenever it is feasible in order to save time.

- ECC on HANA -> S/4HANA on HANA: For systems that already run HANA as a database, the core of the migration is an application upgrade. Here are the options for migrating HANA to the Cloud.

 - Migrate to the cloud firstly with HANA System Replication (HSR) with as little downtime as possible, and then upgrade to S/4HANA

- Backup/restore approach (Prolonged downtime occurs)

- New implementation (Greenfield): If you are creating a new S/4HANA environment, you start it on the HANA database from scratch. You migrate data using ETL tools, SAP Data Services, etc. This is a different approach than migrating from an existing system.

Implications for migration strategy

Software Update Manager (SUM) with DMO: This is the main brownfield migration tool. This tool performs the following tasks:

- Pull data from source databases

- Convert data format if necessary

- Upgrade to S/4HANA and submit data to HANA database at the same time

Using DMO (Database Migration Option) with System Move option, you can perform data extraction in an on-premise environment and the data can be submitted directly to the target system in a cloud environment.

- Pros: Comprehensive process with less manual work

- Cons: High processing load, needs stable network and pre-testing

If you have low network speed, you can export the data files and physically carry them with you, but normally another method is recommended.

It is recommended to test DMO in a sandbox to assess the migration effort and identify problems early on.

SAP migration with the classical heterogeneous system copy

A classic heterogeneous system copy is a migration method based on the export and import of the database through SWPM (Software Provisioning Manager). Export ECC data to a file and load the data to the installed S/4HANA. Since the data structures of ECC and S/4HANA are different, simple data migration is not possible. Due to the structure of the data, even loading the ECC data as is will not function properly after S/4HANA is installed. Thus, the classical way goes like this:

1) Initial migration of only the ECC database to HANA (ECC on HANA enabled and running on on-premise)

2) Migrate to S/4HANA on-premise afterwards

3) Lastly, migrate your systems to the cloud

This method requires several steps, which can lead to greater overall downtime and may require additional hardware. To mitigate the risk, conservative companies decompose the exercise into smaller parts and perform low-risk incremental migrations: first, they migrate to HANA, then they upgrade to S/4HANA, and finally, they migrate to the cloud.

On the other hand, if you are specializing in cloud migration, you may only need to run the final S/4HANA system copy directly in the cloud environment.

Backup/Restore or Replication

Migration of your S/4 system from on-premise to the cloud is possible in multiple ways. A backup approach and restore to HANA on the cloud is an easy method and highly effective as well. While backups can be large, this is viable with shared storage like NFS or a third-party backup tool.

HANA System Replication (HSR)

HSR is a good way in order to reduce downtime. This is a two-phase migration type where one configures the target environment in parallel and replicates from the source HANA (ECC on HANA or S/4 converted on-premise). By this point, the data is in sync, and the takeover is invoked, the target then becomes the primary, and downtime becomes only the takeover time.

But with one-step conversion, beware! So, there is no way to directly replicate the old DB as it has ECC till cutover. Finally, DB-specific replication like e.g., Oracle Data Guard or MSSQL log shipping will not do as the target must be HANA. HSR is mostly relevant for source systems that are HANA.

Cutover Strategy

Whatever process you choose, you need to have a plan in place to reduce business disruption during migration.

- Do most of the heavy lifting data migration before cutover and avoid a lot of last-minute efforts

- Stop any change on the source system and run the final delta load before cutover

- Always have a rollback plan (if the target isn’t working right, delay changes at the source to be able to roll back to the original system if necessary)

Non-production systems

Do Prepare for migration of DEV and QAS Systems. These are more tolerant of downtime, so you might take another approach, perhaps a simple backup/restore or a fresh install and client copy for development. However, in order for testing to be reliable, your QA environment should contain generic data that is representative of production. This is typically done by replicating it after migrating the production back to QA.

Cloud Database Services vs. VMs: SAP HANA

SAP HANA in the Cloud

AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud: all 3 major cloud providers offer SAP HANA on Virtual Machines (VM) or running on bare metal servers. Currently, none of the cloud providers give SAP HANA as a native Platform as a Service (PaaS) because SAP is marketing HANA as a certified appliance. This means you will need your own HANA VMs to manage, including HANA installation, tuning, and high availability, among other operational tasks.

All the cloud providers have mechanisms to deploy HANA.

- AWS: Launch Wizard for SAP helps you automate the deployment of VMs and the installation of HANA in accordance with best practices

- Azure: SAP-specific ARM templates and Azure Center for SAP Solutions (ACSS) for SAP Deployment automation

- Google Cloud: Provides deployment manager scripts for SAP

But those are just tools to help you with implementation, HANA itself runs on a user-managed virtual machine.

Considerations on Data Migration

If your existing ECC system is on a different database (say, SQL Server) and you have DBAs who are familiar with that database, switching to HANA will force you to build new skills. Ensure your partners or team have the HANA administration expertise. This is an important talent consideration, and, don’t forget, the new platform will require training and support.

Integration with other systems

If you have other SAP systems (BI, CRM, etc.) that are not subject to migration but need to be linked, please note the following:

- In cross-DB search scenarios after migration, performance could be affected if these systems are utilizing old DB.

- Ideally, S/4HANA should connect to other systems via interfaces, not direct DB links.

- If you have been using DBLINK, etc. in Oracle, they will no longer function when migrated to HANA.

- You would have to re-design your database, say use HANA's Smart Data Access (SDA) to connect to the external database or use replication.

Summary

The migration of the database into HANA is the most time-consuming and much-effort-requiring phase of any technical migration, so be sure to plan it accordingly and bear in mind the following points:

- Choose the approach to migrate (Single stage SUM DMO or Multi-stage)

- Try in a lower environment to see the performance

- Check that the Cloud Environment (VM size, disk speed) will allow for the migration to finish within the desired downtime

- Modify database-level dependencies (user accounts, synonyms, links, etc.) to reflect the new environment

However, in the core process, the differences on the cloud provider side are rather minor: the vital aspect is which way the transfer of data is done (e.g. AWS Snowball for slow network speeds, Azure Data Box service for physical transport of disks, etc.), but basically, SAP database migration relies on cross-platform tools provided by SAP rather than cloud-specific migration services.

Research exploration on Data migration in production projects

Multiple organizations have migrated from SAP ECC to the S/4HANA cloud. We can learn from these experiences best practices, common challenges, and risk mitigation strategies. Next steps: lessons learned and recommendations from the field.

Key takes from successful SAP migration experiences

Use a pilot environment first

One of the lessons that we learn over and over again from success stories is to run multiple pilot runs, thus have a new copy of your production environment (or part of your data if huge) to have a dress rehearsal performing your migration up in the cloud. The advantage of this is that it is a low-risk environment which will reveal any dependency issues at the earliest opportunity.

For example, if connection errors occur during the pilot, overlooked interfaces can be identified and the dependency list updated prior to production migration. Cognizant reports on a case study of a customer's pilot migration from ECC 6.0 EHP8 to S/4HANA 1909 on AWS, with process refinements prior to production migration.

Working together between business and technology

Dependency analysis depends on the business as much as tech: business process experts have valuable insight into department-centric processes.

For example, if finance says to us, “We upload files into SAP from external systems every month,” that’s a key dependency to know. For example, we were about to miss a third-party tax calculation engine from being called from within SAP, but a finance user asked that we include it, so we did.

To learn more, you can broadly share your migration plan and actively reach out to the departments, asking them if they have any tools or data flows integrated with SAP.

Planning for High Availability and Recovery in the Cloud

Migration to a cloud environment requires careful planning for high availability and resiliency. In the case of Swiss Re's SAP migration to Azure, the existing private cloud environment had frequent failures because high availability was not considered. They did a redesign of high availability with clustering based on SUSE Linux Enterprise High Availability when migrating to Azure. Your migration can become a stepping stone to a better design - this is the main thing you can learn from this case study.

Remove on-premise single points of failure in the cloud. For example:

- Multiple instances of application server

- Create ASCS/ERS failover cluster

- Implement HANA Replication

When planning your cloud-based application, plan for fault tolerance from the start and ensure external dependencies can failover based on your design. For example, using the same load balancer IP even if your backend changes, external services will always have a stable endpoint.

Take advantage of automation and scripting

Will reduce human errors and be more efficient in repetitive tasks. 95% of the infrastructure built on Azure in Swiss Re's projects was automated, including the following:

- Provisioning of network, VM, and storage

- Automation of some SAP installations

- Use Terraform and Azure Resource Manager templates

Automation lets you rapidly and uniformly redeploy or clone environments for testing, as well as ensuring that each environment is built consistently throughout multi-stage migrations like development, QA, and production.

As a best practice, even spend time scripting VM creation, security rules, agent deployment, etc. OS configuration is done with Ansible or PowerShell DSC, and Terraform is typically used for configured Cloud resources.

Making use of existing frameworks and know-how

Consider utilizing frameworks provided by vendors and partners. For example, the following frameworks are available:

- AWS Migration Acceleration Program (MAP)

- Cognizant's frameworks: cCAT, SmartMove

The key thing is, you won’t be starting from zero: SAP and cloud providers have loads of documentation, and partners have playbooks. Look for architecture diagrams and key points (like network configuration and order of steps) from other architecture references and case studies.

Use early run of SAP Readiness Check

So, make sure to have the SAP Readiness Check run from the earliest start of your project concept phases since it will disclose early "show-stoppers" like:

- Incompatible add-ons

- Large data volumes

- Customer-vendor integration (CVI) need

For example, if you need to migrate 20TB but know you really only have 5TB of active data, you could save both downtime and costs by taking on a data archiving project in advance of the migration.

CVI is about merging customer and vendor masters into business partners and is a mandatory requirement of S/4HANA. Doing this can also be a project in and of itself so I want you to at least be aware of that upfront. Many companies conduct these prerequisite projects well in advance of the actual cutover.

Why Integration Testing Is So Helpful

You need to have unit tests, integration tests, user acceptance, and performance tests after migration. You should include integration tests in your plan. For instance, ensure that IDoC submissions from a non-SAP warehouse system to S/4 can be checked that they are processed, or that business users need to verify that reporting from S/4 to other systems comes up as expected.

It is thus the successful migration that is not only judged by whether SAP itself functions, but also how all linked processes behave. In practice, problems such as FTP scripts failing due to DNS changes or firewalls blocking interfaces can occur. These can be discovered by integration testing.

A better method is to prepare a checklist of external interfaces and then test each one methodically as it is available in the cloud environment.

Performance optimization

Following the migration, if you find you are facing performance bottlenecks afterward, it could well be a result of not sizing cloud VMs correctly (too small or wrong resources) or not configuring certain parameters correctly (such as HANA memory allocation and/or Linux kernel settings).

Performing performance testing in a QA environment that replicates your production scale, with SAP standard benchmarks (for instance, dialog response times with ST03N, times for executing batch jobs) and stress-testing critical transactions, is best practice, so that you can fine-tune VM sizes and configuration before going live.

If you have a high latency between the app and the DB in the cloud, you can resolve that by scaling out the application servers or adding more dialog work processes. If you think ahead, resources can scale easily in a cloud environment.

Verifying Data Integrity

Verify that after migration, all data has transferred correctly. Check your Bank balances or pending orders etc. It is important to run reconciliation reports to ensure there is no data loss. This is especially essential if you are moving to the new GL or integrating customers into BP.

It is recommended to run report for comparing trial balance before and after migration, count of specific master data records, etc. It's much easier to make repairs on the fly while the old SAP system is still fresh in your mind (or has read-only access).

Strategies to mitigate dependency analysis

Without knowing that, how much migration can we do? Here are additional better risk mitigation strategies:

Comprehensive stakeholder interviews

It is essential to engage in a dialogue with all stakeholders. Get all teams onboard (Basis, developers, integration team, business owners, infra team, security team, etc. - define this based on your organizations, a short meeting). For this meeting, validate the following:

- What systems are you working within?

- Are there significant jobs?

- Any existing problems (hidden dependencies with manual workarounds)?

Watch out for manual processes that users might not consider "integrations" (a query that downloads a CSV from SAP which you upload to another tool, for example). After migration they also need to be tested.

Allowing buffer time

Add buffers to your project timeline to allow for unknown problems. Even after a full analysis, undiscovered dependencies can be found in testing or in production. For example, it is good to have some time in case it does not have a needed firewall rule.

Use of test tools

Leverage automated testing and code analysis tools:

- Leverage tools like Panaya doing S/4 simulation and detecting errors upfront

- Automate end-to-end testing, including collaboration, using Tricentis and other tools

These tools help identify problems at an early stage and lower the risk of the project.

Consider a Phased Migration

For larger systems, a phased migration may be appropriate.

- Migrate non-production (development, sandboxes) environments to the cloud first - that will allow everyone on your team time to get acclimated

- Closely aligned groups of systems should be lifted/migrated collectively or in a closely coordinated manner

- Identify potential scenarios where you may want to decouple systems (e.g. freezing one while migrating the other)

Develop a rollback plan

Plan ahead to ensure you have a rollback plan if the migration fails.

- Support existing ECC system through Go/No-Go decision

- Prevent or track changes in ECC during cutover

- Run a final backup at the beginning of the cutover so you can restore if needed

Leveraging vendor support

Recognize when you will need to reach out for assistance/expertise/collaboration.

- Engage SAP and discuss the migration plan

- Effective use of SAP expert teams within cloud providers (i.e. AWS, Azure)

- Prepare experts and documentation to troubleshoot any issues encountered in cutover

By using these strategies together, you can substantially minimize dependency issues to ensure a successful cutover.

For complex transitions, use SNPs or similar tools

For migrating complex systems, SNP and CBS are specialized software. These tools can automate not only simple data conversions, but also system splits and consolidations and selective data migrations, and can be rehearsed prior to migration, thus significantly reducing risk. While they may not be significant for simpler migrations, It is important to be aware of the tools available. We've noticed a good example of smooth migration through automation through the use of SNP software, which is introduced in an AWS blog to simulate the migration process and identify problems earlier.

Documentation and Runbooks: Why They Are Important

List all the migration steps, who takes care of them, and how long this will take in a migration runbook. Do make sure you have a step for validating dependency post migration (Example: Step 40: After the migration, check connectivity to XYZ system from S/4 by: Interface team.) It also helps to make sure that everyone on the team is on the same page and that no tasks slip through the cracks in the rush of go-live.

Common challenges and their solutions

Countermeasures to the impact of custom codes

Challenge: Custom code issues may not be discovered during pre-deployment checks, e.g. a little used custom report not being identified in testing which leads to production errors within the S/4 environment.

Solution: Test as many custom functions as you can. Once you have ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) results, start to address those issues in a systematic way and correct the code. If there are any unused custom programs that might cause confusion upon restoration, then consider deleting those first. Be prepared for ABAP developers to debug and correct problems quickly in case they are discovered after the migration. You ensure the new system has developer keys and permissions available for use by developers beginning during production go-live.

Resolving legacy data inconsistencies and data quality

Challenge: S/4HANA may impose more stringent data requirements than ECC (ex: business partner details may become mandatory for information fields that are optional in ECC). Several problems arise due to poor data quality such as Failed Data Conversions, abnormal processing of Transactions etc.

Solution: Execute pre-migration project data cleansing. This is a great time to clean up your vendor/customer master data and delete old historical data. Pre-emptively running SAP consistency checks (for example, from a financial reconciliation standpoint) is also a good idea. Watch out for errors that are reported during the test conversion since some cases (like resetting open postings older than 10 years in test mode) will not raise an issue until you execute the SUM (Software Update Manager) in test mode.

Overcoming constraints of downtime

Challenge: The conversion time (including bulk data migration) for large systems may be higher than the allowable downtime for business continuity.

Solution: Optimizing each step is the key. Consider the following:

- Near-line storage for BW

- Archive for data volume reduction

- Transition to high-speed networks and transporting data physically

- Parallelization of manual export/import tasks

- Optimization of initial load for HANA database

If possible, think about client cloning prior to cutover and migrating the functions in phases. Also, perform the move on holidays or low-traffic hours to create that extra time room. If you want to avoid downtime to the fullest extent, you can perform a two-stage migration and migrate applications first, capturing the changes in transport middleware; that said, this is an advanced process only performed in very specific scenarios - and certainly not common for ECC to S/4HANA migrations!

Changes to the API that are breaking integrations

Challenge: Integration with third-party systems calling RFCs, which are removed or changed in S/4HANA, may no longer work.

Solution:

1) Use the Simplification List and Readiness Check to specify the required integration coordination points

2) No RFC available to read credit card data, migrate to new API or OData service

3) Pre-configure your external systems (change updating schedules) and run full integration tests

4) Short-term you can activate legacy business functionality using SAP notes or compatibility switches, long-term you will need to change to new S/4 processes.

Lack of skills in the team

Challenges: The existing team may not be knowledgeable in terms of cloud environments and S/4HANA.

Solution:

1) Introduce expert training and support.

2) Include cloud architects in the design stage to prevent knowledge loss to infrastructure teams.

3) Educate your team on post-migration operations (checking virtual machines, managing backups in the cloud, etc.).

4) Changes to the S/4HANA system (usage of Fiori and new transaction codes) will be trained to functional users.

5) Include education time in your project plan.

6) While you're building up your internal team, take advantage of the managed services of a partner in the meantime.

Licensing and Compliance

Challenge: Migration to S/4HANA needs re-licensing.

Solution:

1) To get S/4HANA licenses, negotiate early, and validate cloud migration does not breach existing provisions

2) Discuss your licensing needs early with your SAP account manager

3) Verify that any third-party tools that are integrated with SAP are properly licensed for the new environment (change in host hardware may need new licenses)

Need to work together with multiple stakeholders

Challenge: There are a lot of players involved - infrastructure teams, feature teams, cloud providers, SI partners - poor communication can cause delays.

Solution:

1) Build a strong project management structure

2) Define regular checkpoints, clear ownership (RACI matrix) and escalation paths

3) Create a war room (a central workspace, physical or virtual, for the cutover where all stakeholders join)

4) Real-time collaboration using communication tools like Slack, or Teams

5) This establishes the mechanism of rapid cooperation, such as if the interface has a problem after migration, let the network personnel check security rules and let the functional personnel check customization settings.

Indicators of Success for Case Studies

Swiss Re's Azure migration

- After the migration, availability was improved and the migration was accomplished with zero errors

- The secret sauce to success was extensive HA design and testing

- Migrating a large environment of 170 virtual servers required careful planning.

AWS case study about one-step migration

- This integrated approach reduces downtime by 70% and costs by 60%

- Transformation and cloud migration paired together is a winning strategy

- Balance between risk and reward is important: doing everything at once is complex, but minimizes downtime

- Leveraging tools and expertise are key to success

Do a post-migration review to identify what worked and what didn’t for your team to note for future projects.

Setting Up the Technical Environment and Coding

This section provides example configurations and scripts related to dependency analysis and migration. These are general samples and should be adjusted to your environment.

NetApp SnapMirror for Storage replication (Example)

In the case of NetApp SnapMirror for migrating on-premise SAP data to the cloud, the first step is to create a SnapMirror relationship from a source to a destination. Here is an example of a SnapMirror configuration from an on-premise NetApp volume to a cloud NetApp volume (Amazon FSx for ONTAP or Azure NetApp Files).

It assumes, for example, that you have VPN or Direct Connect/ExpressRoute network connectivity, as well as cluster peering and storage VM (SVM) peering.

On the Destination Cluster (cloud side) the command to be executed is:

# Create the SnapMirror relationship from source to destination
# Replace SourceCluster:svm_source with your source cluster and SVM name
# Replace DestCluster:svm_dest with your dest cluster and SVM (if FSx ONTAP, cluster name could be FSx, svm might be fsx volume's svm)
# Here 'vol1' is the volume name on both sides, assuming you will mirror into a volume named vol1 on dest.
FSx-Dest::> snapmirror create \
    -source-path SourceCluster:svm_source_vol1 \
    -destination-path DestCluster:svm_dest_vol1 \
    -type XDP \
    -throttle unlimited

# Initialize the SnapMirror to start the baseline transfer
FSx-Dest::> snapmirror initialize -destination-path DestCluster:svm_dest_vol1

# (After initial sync, you can schedule or manually update:)
# For a one-time manual update (incremental sync):
FSx-Dest::> snapmirror update -destination-path DestCluster:svm_dest_vol1

# To check status of SnapMirror:
FSx-Dest::> snapmirror show -destination-path DestCluster:svm_dest_vol1 -fields state,status,lag-time,last-transfer-end-time

(The above command is based on NetApp ONTAP CLI usage. In the case of FSx for ONTAP, you get the same ONTAP CLI through the management endpoint, while for Azure NetApp Files the replication is handled via the Azure Portal interface or REST APIs. Consult the appropriate documentation for each environment for the precise syntax, but the basic ideas are the same.)

As noted above, SnapMirror can be deployed to facilitate data protection via the steps below:

1) Configure SnapMirror Relationship: Configure XDP(Extended Data Protection) type SnapMirror relationship between source and destination volume

2) Bandwidth Setting: "-throttle unlimited" option will transfer data with maximum bandwidth. If you do not want to occupy network bandwidth, you can limit it by setting the throttling values.

3) Initial data transfer: "snapmirror initialize" command starts an initial copy of the data. This could take some hours or days, depending on the size of the data and bandwidth of the network.

4) Periodic Updates: on a set interval, run "snapmirror update" command to bring changes in source volume to destination volume (if source still in use)

5) Pre-cutover verification: refresh for the last time before cutover to ensure the source and destination have all relations in sync (no lag)

6) Break the SnapMirror relationship: Use the "snapmirror break" command to break the snapmirror relationship, thereby allowing the destination volume to be writable. Once the break is done, the cloud volume becomes a separate copy of the data that can be mounted to the SAP system.

7) Scope: This approach can be used to migrate filesystems (/sapmnt, /usr/sap/SID, etc.) and database files (while quiescing DB or making use of storage consistent snapshots)

SnapMirror Benefits and Cautions

Benefits: SnapMirror maintains file system permissions, timestamps, and all data identically. Once the relationship is dissolved, the SAP instance in the cloud can mount the volume and provide the same data that it had before in an on-premise scenario.

Note:

- Make sure ONTAP version for both environments are compatible.

- A proper network setup (Intercluster LIF) is necessary.

- SnapMirror on NAT environments (FSx ONTAP has a options for public endpoint, support for VPN as well).

- You can read a detailed guide about migrating to FSx for ONTAP using SnapMirror in AWS documentation.

Example: Network security settings for SAP migration

After SAP Migration proper network configuration is required to make sure that all systems can communicate. Here are a few examples of some security settings on AWS and Azure.

AWS environment security settings examples

In AWS, you can do it on security groups to permit specific traffic.

(Inbound permissions configuration for SAP GUI traffic)

The example below shows how to make the SAP application server accessible from the administration host.

# Allow SAP GUI port 3200 from sg-admin to sg-app
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
    --group-id sg-app-group-id \
    --protocol tcp --port 3200 \
    --source-group sg-admin-group-id

The above command means:

- sg-app: Security group for SAP application servers

- TCP/3200: SAP GUI communication dispatcher port

- sg-admin: Security group for admin/bastion hosts

You can use the security group ID to ensure that connectivity remains intact even when the IP address of your administrative host changes, or you can provide a CIDR IP range to grant access to your corporate LAN subnets, e.g. if your users are connecting via VPN from on-premise.

(Access permission settings from external systems)

Below is an example to enable an on-premise API server (with IP as 10.0.5.5) to connect to an OData service on SAP system via HTTPS.

aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
    --group-id sg-app-group-id \
    --protocol tcp --port 443 \
    --cidr 10.0.5.5/32

It will permit that particular IP - you can mix rules as you like and/or use source security groups as needed.

Azure environment security settings examples

In Azure the equivalent traffic control is done by Network Security Groups (NSGs).

When creating NSG rules using the Azure CLI, the following configuration is required:

- Target: “SAP-App-NSG” applied to SAP subnets

- Purpose: To grant access from the on-premise subnet (10.0.5.0/24) to the following

 - SAP Dispatcher (port 3200-3299)

 - RFC Gateway (port 3300)

az network nsg rule create \
   --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
   --nsg-name SAP-App-NSG \
   --name Allow_SAPGUI_RFC_From_OnPrem \
   --priority 100 \
   --direction Inbound --access Allow --protocol Tcp \
   --source-address-prefixes 10.0.5.0/24 \
   --destination-address-prefixes VirtualNetwork \
   --destination-port-ranges 3200-3300

Here we establish the priority (lower shows higher priority in Azure). This'll open TCP on ports 3200 - 3300 from the source subnet 10.0.5.0/24 (could be your on-premise network via Expressroute) to any destination in your Virtual Network (practically virtually allowing the SAP VMs since the NSG is on the SAP subnet). Here we use VirtualNetwork as destination which means address space of our VNet (virtual network).

Azure also allows for more flexible configuration with Application Security Groups (ASG). With ASG tags, the command can be simplified to:

--destination-asgs SAP-App
--source-asgs AdminHosts

An ASG gives you the ability to apply security rule sets to specific applications or hosts.

Example of security configuration in a Google Cloud environment (Setting firewall rules with gcloud)

Here is an example of opening a firewall rule in Google Cloud to allow access to an SAP system.

gcloud compute firewall-rules create allow-sapgui-from-onprem \
   --network my-sap-vpc \
   --direction INGRESS --action ALLOW --rules tcp:3200-3200 \
   --source-ranges 10.0.5.0/24 \
   --target-tags sap-app-servers

- Basic setup: Tag your VMs with something like “sap-app-servers” and allow access from your on-premise subnet

- Syntax: For Google Cloud, specify port range in the format --rules tcp:port-port

Based on the dependency analysis, the network rules should be defined and should also recognize any interface and user that reaches SAP. The following ports must open on your SAP system:

- SAP dispatcher: 3200 (in case of instance 00)

- SAP RFC Gateway: 3300

- ICM HTTP/HTTPS: 50000 (HTTP), 50001 (HTTPS) (in case of instance 00)

- HANA SQL port: 30015 (tenant DB), 30013 (system DB, for HANA instance 00)

- Administration: SSH (22) or RDP (3389)

- SAPRouter port: Usually 3299 (if in use)

- IDoc/OTC: through a gateway or a designated port

Documenting all necessary ports to facilitate smooth migration and the mapping of those ports in your cloud security configuration during system startup will ensure that your systems can communicate. This will ensure system availability and security.

(Example script) Script to automate validation and check for dependencies

Automating dependency verification is important in SAP system migration efforts. This text presents a practical method for verifying external connections of SAP application servers.

Example 1: Checking active connections (Linux)

On the Linux SAP server, you can run the below command to check all TCP/UDP connections/listening ports.

ss -tupan > connections.txt

This command will filter out local connections and print external connection info with the process name. If you just take a look at the log, you will read, "Gateway process has established a connection to 10.9.8.7 on port 443", for instance. Then, ensure that these IP addresses can be reached in your cloud environment (e.g. via VPN), as well as DNS resolution.

ESTAB 0 0 10.1.2.3:50000 10.9.8.7:443  ...  users:(("gwrd",pid=1234,fd=50))

To obtain a list of connections, execute the following command:

ss -tun | grep ESTAB | awk '{print $5}' | sort | uniq -c

The unique destination addresses of the established connections are listed with the respective amount of connections to each one so that you can easily tell if the server has many connections and you can gain a better understanding of the dependencies.

Example 2: ping test script (Bash)

Run the command below to see if you are able to connect to the hosts found in the dependencies analysis:

HOSTS=("saprouter.mydomain.com" "sftp.partner.com" "10.0.5.10" "mail.server.local")
for h in "${HOSTS[@]}"; do
  echo "Pinging $h..."
  ping -c 4 "$h" &> /dev/null
  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "Success: $h is reachable"
  else
    echo "Error: Cannot reach $h"
  fi
done

The script iterates over each host in the list and pings to make sure that there is network connectivity.

Note:

- Some servers may not respond to ICMP (ping)

- In cases like these, we encourage you to use netcat (nc) to test TCP connectivity instead

Example 3: Test TCP Port Connectivity
check_port() {
  local host=$1
  local port=$2
  nc -z -w 5 $host $port
  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "$host:$port is open"
  else
    echo "Cannot connect to $host:$port"
  fi
}

check_port "sftp.partner.com" 22
check_port "saprouter.mydomain.com" 3299
check_port "10.0.5.10" 443

netcat can be used in zero I/O mode to easily check the possibility of establishing a TCP connection.

- -w 5 option: Define the timeout for 5 seconds

- Add this command to a script that you can run to check connectivity to all essential ports.

If you are using Windows-based systems, the power shell provides Test-NetConnection cmdlet which helps you check the status of a connection with the given host and port.

Example 4: Automated ABAP Dependency Checking (ATC) with RFC Scripts

If you want to run ATC checks remotely, you can script them: SAP offers ATC batch execution tasks, which can be executed directly in the system or by using the Custom Code Migration app in Solution Manager to automate them.

(Infrastructure as Code example)

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) snippets (e.g., Terraform, ARM templates) allow you to define the structure of your environment. For instance, you can use Terraform to define AWS security groups in a declarative and reusable manner to maintain consistency in your cloud environment.

resource "aws_security_group" "sap_app_sg" {
  name   = "SAP-App-SG"
  vpc_id = var.vpc_id

  ingress {
    description      = "Allow SAP GUI from Admin hosts"
    from_port        = 3200
    to_port          = 3299
    protocol         = "tcp"
    security_groups  = [aws_security_group.admin_sg.id]
  }
  ingress {
    description      = "Allow HTTPS from corporate network"
    from_port        = 443
    to_port          = 443
    protocol         = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks      = ["10.0.5.0/24"]
  }
  # egress is usually open by default to all (0.0.0.0/0) for outgoing
  tags = {
    Name = "SAP Application Security Group"
  }
}
Other configuration examples

SUSE Cluster Configuration for SAP (Optional): In HA clustering implementations (for ASCS+ERS or HANA, etc.), a virtual IP configuration must be in place to ensure that SUSE Cluster Configuration for SAP stays the same in the event of a failover. With this arrangement, system continuity is maintained following a failure. Swiss Re, for instance, clustered SAP with SUSE HA Extension in Azure to maintain consistency and ensure API endpoints do not change when a failover occurs. In a typical configuration, you define a cluster resource for the IP and then configure the SAP resources as dependent on it. For a virtual IP or DNS connected by external systems, you'll need to configure that on the cluster itself (eg, /etc/ctdb/public_addresses or pacemaker resources). SAP S/4HANA systems are critical to business processes and need to be highly available. It is simply the idea of clustering, where if one of the machines fails, another machine can take its place and automatically continue service, and users are not aware that any interruption occurred. SUSE Linux Enterprise Server for SAP Applications is optimized and certified by SAP for SAP HANA, SAP S/4HANA, and SAP NetWeaver. It helps you to configure and implement multiple HA/DR scenarios with enqueue failover and recovery options.

HANA parameters tuning: After the migration, it is advisable to examine your HANA parameter settings to ensure you are using your VM RAM as efficiently as possible. In particular, tuning global_allocation_limit to be around 70-80% of physical memory and ensuring that auto_log_backup is enabled to prevent the log disk from getting full. It is a critical operational step to keep your database healthy. You can check the status of your HANA memory by using a simple SQL statement:

SELECT HOST, TOTAL_MEMORY_USED_SIZE, INSTANCE_TOTAL_MEMORY_ALLOCATED_SIZE 
FROM M_HOST_RESOURCE_UTILIZATION;

You can do this with this query: It will show you how much memory is given and how much of it is in use.

SAP Profile Adjustment: If the hostname or instance number changes, the instance profile and domain configuration must be updated. For example, if the SID instance number has changed in a cloud environment, make sure all RFC connections are updated to point to the new host/instance. An excellent idea would be to script bulk SM59 connections check.

With these settings and scripts, you can embed them in migration playbooks to automate dependency management and validation, providing a means to centrally document what has to be configured and the code to implement and test those specifications.

Summary

SAP S/4HANA Migration from On-Premise to Cloud

The migration from on-premises SAP ECC 6.0 to SAP S/4HANA in the cloud requires careful planning. The key to success is an infrastructure and application dependency analysis. By identifying all of the network, storage, compute and security dependencies, as well as understanding how all of your applications interact, smooth migration can be ensured.

Dependency Analysis is Critical

- Avoid dropped connections and performance problems

- Dependency mapping with tools like SAP Readiness Check

- Networking, security, storage, and databases are approached on AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud differently

Practical Implementation Examples

- Data Replication using NetApp SnapMirror

- Creating security rules

- Scripting for connectivity testing

Important Points for Cloud Migration

- This is still the same fundamental idea: your SAP system and its components must communicate and work correctly

- Installations in the cloud let you benefit from new methods (security groups, managed storage services, etc.)

- If you plan and analyze it step by step, you can make your SAP systems resilient and productive

S/4HANA migration isn’t just a technical upgrade but an opportunity to re-architect your infrastructure for scalability, agility, and future-proofing. With the complete dependencies analysis and application of the best practices listed here will help ensure a successful Migration Project.

entry.title: Configuring SAP S/4HANA for Diverse Withholding Tax Scenarios
entry.content:
Withholding Tax (WHT), also known as Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) in some countries like India, is a crucial aspect of tax compliance. Businesses need to accurately deduct and remit taxes on payments made to vendors and other parties. SAP S/4HANA offers robust functionalities to configure and manage various withholding tax scenarios, ensuring adherence to legal requirements and streamlining tax processes.

In some jurisdictions, the obligation to deduct withholding tax arises at the earliest of two events:

  • Upon Credit: When the amount is credited to the partner’s account, encompassing capital accounts.
  • Upon Payment: When the payment is actually made to the partner, regardless of the method (cash, cheque, draft, or other means).

This blog post delves into different withholding tax scenarios, with a particular focus on the scenario where deduction occurs at the earlier of credit or payment, detailing the necessary customization steps for configuring these scenarios in SAP S/4HANA.

1. Formula Based withholding tax Calculation

In scenarios where withholding tax rates vary based on progressive slabs, SAP S/4HANA offers the formula-based withholding tax calculation feature.

Scenario: Calculate withholding tax based on the following annual income slabs:

  • Up to ₹ 3,000: 15%
  • ₹ 3,001 to ₹ 10,000: 22%
  • Above ₹ 10,001: 34%

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA:

A. Define withholding tax Code:

  • Navigate to IMG -> Extended withholding tax -> Calculation -> withholding tax Codes -> Define withholding tax Codes (or use transaction SPRO).
  • Create a new withholding tax code.
  • Crucially, activate the "W.tax form" flag (Technical field V_T059Z-XQFOR). This indicates that the calculation will be formula-based.

B. Define Formulas for Calculating withholding tax:

  • Go to IMG -> Extended withholding tax -> Calculation -> Define Formulas for Calculating withholding tax.
  • Create a new formula.
  • Enter the Validity date and Currency of the Company Code.
  • Define the slabs by entering the "Amount from", "Amount to", and the corresponding "Percentage". For the given scenario, you would define three steps in the formula. You can also define Reductions (optional) if applicable.

Amount from: 0, Amount to: 3000, Percentage: 15.

Amount from: 3001, Amount to: 10000, Percentage: 22

Amount from: 10001, (leave "Amount to" blank for infinity), Percentage: 34

System Behavior: When an invoice with this withholding tax code is processed:

  • The system will apply the defined formula to the withholding tax base amount.
  • It will calculate the withholding tax based on the progressive slabs defined in the formula.

 Example Calculation (Base Amount ₹ 12,000):

Using the defined formula, the calculation would be: (₹ 3,000 * 15%) + ((₹ 10,000 - ₹ 3,000) * 22%) + ((₹ 12,000 - ₹ 10,000) * 34%) = ₹ 450 + ₹ 1540 + ₹ 680 = ₹ 2670

 

2. TAN Based Exemption Limit Check (India Localization)

In India, businesses with a Tax Deduction and Collection Account Number (TAN) can obtain exemption certificates for certain vendors, allowing for withholding tax deduction only on the amount exceeding a specified limit. This exemption is often managed at the Permanent Account Number (PAN) level of the seller.

Scenario: withholding tax Section 194Q (withholding tax on purchase of goods) mandates withholding tax at 0.1% on the amount exceeding ₹ 50 lakh in a financial year from a seller if the total purchases from that seller exceed ₹ 50 lakh. This check needs to consider cumulative purchases across all vendors associated with the same seller PAN.

Configuration Steps in SAP ERP

A. Withholding tax Type for Invoice: Do not activate the standard accumulation functionality. Set the "Accumulation" to "No Accumulation".

B. Withholding tax Code for Invoice: Ensure that the "Calculation of withholding tax according to formula" checkbox is unchecked.

C. Mapping of Invoice and Payment WHT Types and Codes:

Map the Invoice time withholding tax type and code with the corresponding Payment time withholding tax type and code. This ensures consistency in exemption limit checks across both stages.

 

 

D. Maintain PAN in Vendor Master:

In the General Data view of the vendor master, under the "Vendor: Country-Spec. Enh." tab, maintain the PAN of the vendor.

E. Maintain TAN Exemption Data in Vendor Master (Company Code View):

Navigate to the Company Code view of the vendor master, under the "Vendor: Country-Spec. Enh." -> "India: TAN-Based Exemption" tab. Enter the following details:

  • Section code/ section code group (e.g., 194Q)
  • Certificate number issued by the tax authorities.
  • Exemption rate (the percentage of withholding tax exempted).
  • Exemption from and exemption to date (validity period of the certificate).
  • Reason for exemption.
  • Withholding tax code and tax type (for both invoice and payment).
  • Exemption threshold (e.g., 5000000 for ₹ 50 lakh). Ensure the same threshold is maintained for both invoice and payment types mapped to the same certificate number, even for duplicate vendors with the same certificate.
  • Currency.

System Behavior:

  • The system tracks the cumulative amount paid/credited across all vendors linked to the same PAN.
  • Once this cumulative amount exceeds the defined exemption threshold, withholding tax will be deducted on the excess amount for subsequent invoices or payments, considering the defined exemption rate.

 

3. Withholding Tax Grossing Up

Grossing up occurs when the payer (buyer) agrees to pay the seller (payee) a specific net amount and also covers the withholding tax liability on behalf of the seller. Essentially, the payer "grosses up" the payment so that after deducting the withholding tax, the seller receives the agreed-upon net amount.

In some countries, like Thailand, the concept of withholding tax grossing up is used. Here, the payer bears the withholding tax on behalf of the payee, resulting in the payee receiving the full invoice amount.

Scenario: A business needs to pay a vendor THB 1,000, and also pay the 5% withholding tax on the vendor's behalf.

Reason: This is often used in situations like:

  • Executive compensation or bonuses where the company wants the employee to receive a specific net amount.
  • Cross-border payments where the payer wants to ensure the foreign recipient receives the full intended amount without the burden of local withholding tax.

Accounting Impact: The payer accounts for the expense at a grossed-up value (net amount + withheld tax). The payment to the vendor is the agreed-upon net amount. The withheld tax is paid separately by the payer to the tax authorities, but it's treated as an additional cost/expense for the payer, not a deduction from the vendor's payment.

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA:

A. Define withholding tax Code:

Navigate to IMG -> Extended withholding tax -> Calculation -> withholding tax Codes -> Define withholding tax Codes.

For the relevant withholding tax code, maintain the "Posting indicator" value as "2". This setting signifies that the tax amount should be posted to a separate expense account instead of reducing the vendor payment.

B. Define Accounts for withholding tax for "Gross.Up" Offsetting Entry:

Navigate to IMG -> Extended withholding tax -> Posting -> Define Accounts for withholding tax. 

For the relevant withholding tax type and code, configure the "Expense account for withholding tax". This is where the grossed-up tax amount will be debited.

 

System Behavior: When an invoice subject to gross-up is posted:

  • The system will calculate the withholding tax amount.
  • The accounting entry will reflect the following (as per the example):
    • Debit: Business partner (full invoice amount - ₹ 1,000)
    • Debit: Withholding tax expense (grossed-up tax amount - ₹ 52.63)
    • Credit: Bank (total payment - ₹ 1,000)
    • Credit: Withholding tax payable (tax liability - ₹ 52.63)

The system effectively pays the vendor the full amount while also accounting for the withholding tax expense separately. The grossed-up amount for tax calculation is derived such that applying the WHT rate to it yields the desired tax amount. In the example, if the invoice is ₹ 1,000 and the WHT rate is 5%, the grossed-up amount is ₹ 1,052.63 (₹ 1,000 / (1 - 0.05)), and 5% of ₹ 1,052.63 is approximately ₹ 52.63.

 

Understanding Accumulation/Threshold Limit Checks

A fundamental concept in withholding tax is the accumulation/threshold limit check. This mechanism determines when withholding tax becomes applicable based on the total amount paid or credited to a supplier within a specific period. SAP S/4HANA, similar to SAP ECC, traditionally performs this check at the vendor master level.

Let's explore different variations of this check:

4. Accumulation Limit Check at Monthly Level

In certain scenarios, like rent payments, withholding tax might be applicable only when the monthly payment exceeds a specific threshold.

Scenario: Deduct withholding tax on rent payments only when the monthly rent exceeds ₹ 50,000.

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

A. Set Accumulation Type:

  • Navigate to the Define withholding tax Type for Invoice Posting activity (ID: 104225).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key (e.g., IN for India).
  • Locate the withholding tax type relevant to your requirement (e.g.: Section 194I (Rent)).
  • Set the "Accumulation type" to "Per month". This instructs the system to track payments monthly for this WHT type.
  • Verify that the corresponding withholding tax code(s) for your withholding tax section (e.g. 194I) have the correct withholding tax rate defined.

B. Set Minimum Amount:

  • Open the Define Min/Max Amounts for withholding tax Codes activity (ID: 103915).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Find the withholding tax code associated with Section 194I.
  • Set the "Minimum base amount" to 50000 and the "Valid from date" to 01.04.2025 (assuming the new fiscal year starts then).

C. Vendor Master Assignment:

Ensure the relevant withholding tax code for 194I is assigned to the respective vendors in the Vendor Master data.

System Behaviour: When a rent invoice is entered for a vendor with this configuration:

  • The system will check the total rent paid to that vendor within the current month.
  • If the cumulative monthly rent exceeds ₹ 50,000, withholding tax will be calculated on the entire amount of the current invoice.

 

5. Accumulation Check at Transaction Level

In some cases, withholding tax applicability depends on the value of a single transaction, irrespective of the total amount paid to the vendor.

Scenario: Deduct withholding tax when a single transaction amount for lottery or game winnings is ₹ 10,000 or more (Section 194B).

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

A. Set No Accumulation:

  • Go to the Define withholding tax Type for Invoice Posting activity (ID: 104225).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Find the withholding tax type for Section 194B.
  • Set the "Accumulation" field to "No accumulation". This ensures each transaction is evaluated independently.

B. Set Minimum Amount:

  • Open the Define Min/Max Amounts for withholding tax Codes activity (ID: 103915).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Locate the withholding tax code for Section 194B.
  • Set the "Minimum base amount" to 10000 and the "Valid from date" to 01.04.2025.

C. Vendor Master Linkage: Ensure the appropriate withholding tax code for 194B is linked to the relevant vendors in the Vendor Master.

System Behavior:

When a payment is made for lottery or game winnings:

  • The system will evaluate the amount of that specific payment.
  • If the single transaction amount is ₹ 10,000 or more, withholding tax will be deducted.

 

6. Accumulation Limit Check at Financial Year Level

With evolving tax regulations, new sections might be introduced with accumulation based on the financial year.

Scenario: Implement withholding tax under the newly introduced Section 194T, applicable to payments (remuneration, interest, commission) made by a firm to its partners, where withholding tax at 10% is levied if the total payment exceeds ₹ 20,000 in a financial year (starting April).

Configuration Steps in SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

A. Define withholding tax Key:

  • Access the Define withholding tax Keys configuration activity (ID: 104229).
  • Create a new entry for 194T with a relevant description (e.g., "withholding tax on Partner Payments").

B. Define withholding tax Type:

  • Use the Define withholding tax Type for Invoice Posting activity (ID: 104225).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Create a new withholding tax type for 194T.
  • Set the "Accumulation type" to "Per year starting with month: 4". (for Apr to Mar FY) This configures the system to track payments annually, starting from April.

C. Define withholding tax Code:

  • Create a new withholding tax code specifically for 194T.
  • Set the tax rate to 10%.
  • Assign the withholding tax key (194T) and the newly created withholding tax type to this code.

D. Set Minimum Amount:

  • Open the Define Min/Max Amounts for withholding tax Codes activity (ID: 103915).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Locate the withholding tax code for 194T.
  • Set the "Minimum base amount" to 20000 and the "Valid from date" to 01.04.2025.

E. Payment Posting WHT Type and Code (Optional): If required by your business processes, similarly create a corresponding withholding tax type and withholding tax code for payment postings related to Section 194T.

F. Vendor Master Assignment: In the Vendor Master for partners (treated as vendors), ensure the newly created withholding tax code(s) for 194T are added to their records.

System Behavior: When processing invoices or payments to partners:

  • The system will track the cumulative amount paid to that partner within the financial year (April to March).
  • Once the total exceeds ₹ 20,000, withholding tax at 10% will be deducted on the subsequent payments.

Conclusion

Configuring SAP S/4HANA for different withholding tax scenarios requires a thorough understanding of the specific legal requirements and careful implementation of the system's functionalities. By correctly setting up accumulation types, threshold limits, formula-based calculations, TAN-based exemptions, and gross-up mechanisms, businesses can ensure accurate withholding tax deductions, comply with regulations, and streamline their tax processes within the SAP environment. Remember to always test your configurations thoroughly in a non-production environment before deploying them in your live system.

Read more at the SAP Help Portal. Subscribe to this blog post to get informed about updates automatically.

entry.title: Indian Budget 2025: Managing the Omission of TCS Section 206C(1H) in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
The Budget 2025 has brought a significant relief by removing the TCS provision under Section 206C(1H) on the sale of goods, effective from April 1, 2025. Previously, sellers were required to collect TCS if the aggregate value of goods sold exceeded ₹ 50 lakhs, subject to other conditions. This often led to overlaps and compliance complexities with the TDS provision under Section 194Q, where buyers had to deduct TDS on similar transactions.

Section

Before 1st April 2025

From 1st April 2025

206C(1H) - Purchase of Goods

50,00,000

Nil (No TCS Applicable)

This blog post outlines the necessary steps within the FI and SD modules of SAP to ensure your system reflects this change and ceases TCS calculation for relevant transactions from the specified date.

 

Delimiting TCS Calculation in SAP FI Module

This change necessitates adjustments in your FI module of SAP S/4HANA (On-prem and Cloud) system to ensure compliance and avoid errors. Follow these recommendations:

A. Tax Codes with Only TCS 206C(1H):

  • Discontinue the use of any tax codes in your SAP system that exclusively contain the TCS rate under Section 206C(1H) starting from April 1, 2025.
  • Modify the description of these tax codes to clearly indicate "Not Applicable from April 1st, 2025" to prevent accidental selection by users.

B. Tax Codes with Combined GST and TCS 206C(1H):

  • If you have existing tax codes that combine GST rates with the TCS rate under Section 206C(1H), prioritize using separate tax codes containing only the applicable GST rates.
  • Create new tax codes specifically for GST rates if they don't already exist. This approach ensures clarity and simplifies future tax rate adjustments.
  • Similar to point 1, update the description of the combined GST and TCS tax codes to indicate "Not Applicable from April 1st, 2025".

C. Avoid Modifying Existing Tax Codes with TCS:

  • Refrain from directly modifying the TCS rates within existing tax codes that have been used in past financial postings. This can lead to inconsistencies when reversing documents posted before April 1, 2025.
  • During year-end closing activities in April or May, you might need to post documents with posting dates prior to April 1, 2025. The original tax codes with TCS will be required for these historical postings.
  • Altering previously used tax codes can compromise the integrity of your financial records.

 

Delimiting TCS Calculation in SAP SD Module

Here are the steps to delimit TCS calculation in your SAP SD system:

A. Modify the Validity End Date of TCS Condition Records:

The TCS calculation in SAP SD is typically managed through condition records maintained in pricing procedures. You need to identify and update the validity of these condition records to reflect the discontinuation of TCS collection from April 1, 2025.

  • Identify TCS Condition Names: The standard condition names for TCS might be JTC1 and JTCB. However, these might be customized in your system. To verify the correct condition names mapped to your pricing procedure(s), consult the table J_1IEXCDEFN.
  • Update Condition Records: Once you have identified the correct condition names, execute transaction VK12 (Change Condition). For each identified TCS condition record, modify the "Validity to" date to 31st March 2025.

Note: Ensure you perform this step for all relevant pricing procedures where TCS is currently configured.

B. Verify Copy Control Settings for Redetermination of Taxes:

When creating billing documents with reference to preceding documents (like deliveries or sales orders), the system's copy control settings determine how pricing conditions are handled. To ensure that the updated validity of the TCS condition records is considered, you need to verify that the pricing type is set to redetermine taxes.

  • Access Copy Control Transactions: Navigate to the appropriate copy control transaction based on the preceding document:
    • VTFL: For invoices created with reference to a delivery.
    • VTFA: For invoices created with reference to a sales order.
    • VTFF: For invoices created with reference to another billing document.
    • (Choose the relevant transaction code(s) like B, C, or G based on your specific business scenarios).
  • Check Pricing Type: Within the copy control settings for the relevant item category mapping, verify the "Pricing type" field. If it is set to "Fix values" or "Unchanged", the system will copy the JTC1 condition value from the source document without redetermining it. This could lead to unwanted TCS calculation even after March 31, 2025, if the source document was created before this date.
  • Adjust Pricing Type (If Necessary): If the pricing type is set to "Fix values" or "Unchanged", consider changing it to a pricing type that includes tax redetermination (e.g., 'B' - Carry out new pricing). Evaluate the impact of this change on other pricing elements before making the modification.

C. Manage Pricing Dates for Invoices in the Current Financial Year (April 2025 onwards):

For invoices that you wish to post in the current financial year (starting April 1, 2025) and onwards, you need to ensure that the pricing date passed to the billing document is on or after 01.04.2025. This will ensure that the system does not consider the (now expired) TCS condition records during pricing.

  • Impact of Goods Issue Date: Be aware that if the goods issue date (or service rendered date) in the preceding document was within the validity period of the TCS condition records (i.e., before or on 31st March 2025), this date might be proposed as the pricing date in the billing document, potentially leading to TCS calculation.
  • Modifying Pricing Date: In such scenarios, you will need to modify the pricing date in the billing document (transaction VF02) to a date on or after 01.04.2025 to avoid TCS calculation.
  • Pricing Date Control: The default determination of the pricing date in the billing document is controlled by the settings you have maintained for the pricing date field in transaction V/06 (Sales Document Item Category). Ensure your configuration aligns with your requirement to avoid unwanted TCS calculation. You might need to review and potentially adjust this configuration based on your business needs.

 

Additional Information

For users of the S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, refer to SAP Note 3576728 for detailed steps and considerations related to this abolition. Similarly, customers utilizing S/4HANA On-premise environments should consult SAP Note 3574318 for the necessary adjustments within their systems to reflect the removal of this TCS requirement.

 

Conclusion

By carefully following these steps, you can effectively delimit the TCS calculation in your SAP FI and SD modules from April 1, 2025. Remember to thoroughly test these changes in a non-production environment before implementing them in your live system to ensure a smooth transition and accurate billing processes. Always consult with your SAP consultants for any complex configurations or specific business requirements.

entry.title: Indian Budget 2025: Navigating TDS Changes in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
The Indian Budget 2025 has brought forth significant amendments to the Tax Deduction at Source (TDS) provisions under the Income Tax Act, 1961. These changes aim to simplify tax compliance for both businesses and individuals by introducing higher threshold limits and streamlining overall taxation processes.

This article will delve into the some of the key changes in TDS and provide a practical guide on how to implement these updated requirements within your SAP S/4HANA system.

 

Enhanced Threshold Limits For TDS

A crucial aspect of the Budget 2025 is the increase in threshold limits for TDS applicability under certain sections. This means that TDS will only be triggered when the transaction value exceeds these new, higher limits. The revised thresholds, effective from April 1, 2025, are outlined below:

Section

Before 1st April 2025

From 1st April 2025

194B - Winnings from lottery, crossword puzzle Etc.

194BB - Winnings from horse race

Aggregate of amounts exceeding 10,000/- during the financial year

10,000/- in respect of a single transaction

194-I - Rent

2,40,000 (in a financial year)

50,000 per month

Let's explore how to configure SAP S/4HANA to align with these enhanced threshold limits for specific TDS sections:

For TDS Section 194I (Rent Payments)

To ensure TDS is deducted only when monthly rent payments exceed ₹ 50,000, follow these customization steps in SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

Set Accumulation Type:

  1. Navigate to the Define Withholding Tax Type for Invoice Posting activity (ID: 104225).
  2. Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  3. Locate the withholding tax type for 194I.
  4. Set the "Accumulation type" to "Per month". This instructs the system to track payments on a monthly basis.
  5. Verify that the corresponding withholding tax code(s) for 194I have the correct TDS rate.

 

Set Minimum Amount:

  1. Open the Define Min/Max Amounts for Withholding Tax Codes activity (ID: 103915).
  2. Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  3. Find the withholding tax code for your 194I.
  4. Set the "Minimum base amount" to INR 50,000 and the "Valid from date" to 01.04.2025.

Ensure the relevant withholding tax code is assigned to the respective vendors in the Vendor Master. Now, upon entering a rent invoice, the SAP S/4HANA system will:

  • Check if the total rent paid to the vendor within that specific month has surpassed ₹ 50,000.
  • If the threshold is crossed, TDS will be calculated on the entire amount of that month's invoice.

 

For TDS Section 194B (Lottery/Game Winnings):

To deduct TDS when a single winning amount from lottery or games is ₹ 10,000 or more, implement the following configurations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

Set No Accumulation:

  1. Go to the Define Withholding Tax Type for Invoice Posting activity (ID: 104225).
  2. Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  3. Find the withholding tax type for 194B.
  4. Set the "Accumulation" to "No accumulation". This ensures that each transaction is evaluated independently against the threshold.

Set Minimum Amount:

  1. Open the Define Min/Max Amounts for Withholding Tax Codes activity (ID: 103915).
  2. Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  3. Locate the withholding tax code for your 194B.
  4. Set the "Minimum base amount" to 10,000 and the "Valid from date" to 01.04.2025.
  5. Ensure the appropriate withholding tax code is linked to the relevant vendors in the Vendor Master.

With this configuration, the system will:

  • Evaluate each payment for lottery or game winnings individually.
  • Deduct TDS if the amount of a single transaction is ₹ 10,000 or more.

  

TDS on Partner’s Remuneration Section-194T

A significant addition in the Budget 2025 is the introduction of Section 194T, which mandates TDS on certain payments made by a firm (partnership firm or LLP) to its partners. Previously, TDS was not applicable to payments like salary, bonus, commission, or interest to partners.

Key Provisions of Section 194T:

Applicability: Any firm responsible for paying salary, remuneration, commission, bonus, or interest to a partner.

TDS Rate: 10%.

TDS Threshold Limit: TDS is applicable only if the aggregate amount paid or credited to a partner during the financial year exceeds ₹ 20,000.

Timing of TDS Deduction: TDS must be deducted at the earlier of the following two events:

  • At the Time of Credit: When the sum is credited to the partner’s account, including capital accounts.
  • At the Time of Payment: When the payment is made to the partner, whether through cash, cheque, draft, or other modes.

Payment

TDS Rate

TDS Threshold Limit

Remuneration, Interest and Commission paid to partners

10%

Exceeds Rs. 20,000 in a financial year.

Steps to Configure 194T in SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

A. Define Withholding Tax Key:

  • Access the Define Withholding Tax Keys configuration activity (ID: 104229).
  • Create a new entry for 194T with a relevant description.

B. Define Withholding Tax Type:

  • Use the Define Withholding Tax Type for Invoice Posting activity (ID: 104225).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Create a new withholding tax type for 194T.
  • Set the "Accumulation type" to "Per year starting with month: 4". This ensures annual tracking of payments from April to March.

 

C. Define Withholding Tax Code:

  • Create a new withholding tax code specifically for 194T.
  • Set the tax rate to 10%.
  • Assign the withholding tax key and type created in the previous steps.

D. Set Minimum Amount:

  • Open the Define Min/Max Amounts for Withholding Tax Codes activity (ID: 103915).
  • Choose Configure and select your country/region key.
  • Locate the withholding tax code for 194T.
  • Set the "Minimum base amount" to INR 20,000 and the "Valid from date" to 01.04.2025.

E. Similarly, create a corresponding withholding tax type and withholding tax code for payment postings, if required by your business processes.

F. In the Vendor Master (for partners treated as vendors), ensure the newly created withholding tax code(s) for 194T are added to the partner's record.

 

Exemption Limit vs. Accumulation/Threshold Limit in TDS (India)

In the context of Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) in India, it's crucial to distinguish between how the "exemption limit" and the "accumulation/threshold limit" are applied. These determine when and how TDS is triggered.

Exemption Limit Check:

  • Concept: Under an exemption limit check, TDS is levied only on the amount that exceeds a specified limit. Any cumulative amount paid or credited to a supplier beyond this limit becomes subject to TDS deduction.
  • SAP Implementation (India Localization): In SAP S/4HANA and SAP ECC, the exemption limit check, as described, operates specifically for TAN-based exemptions at the vendor-PAN level. This means:
    • The exemption limit is defined at the vendor master level under the "Country India" tab, based on the exemption certificate number.
    • The system tracks the cumulative amount paid/credited across all vendors associated with the same Permanent Account Number (PAN).
    • Once the cumulative amount across these vendors exceeds the specified exemption limit, TDS is deducted on the excess amount for subsequent invoices or payments.
  • Example (TDS Section 194Q): As highlighted, Section 194Q stipulates that TDS at 0.1% is applicable on the amount exceeding ₹50 lakh in a financial year from a seller from whom purchases worth more than ₹50 lakh have been made.
    • If a buyer with multiple vendors under the same seller PAN cumulatively purchases goods worth ₹55 lakh, TDS will be deducted on ₹5 lakh (₹55 lakh - ₹50 lakh) on the subsequent invoice or payment.

 

Accumulation/Threshold Limit Check:

  • Concept: With an accumulation/threshold limit check, TDS becomes applicable the moment the total amount paid or credited to a supplier in a financial year crosses a predefined threshold. Once this threshold is breached, TDS is deducted on the entire amount paid or credited to that specific supplier for the financial year.
  • SAP Implementation: At present in SAP S/4HANA and SAP ECC, the accumulation/threshold limit check is performed at the individual vendor level, irrespective of how many vendor masters exist for the same PAN.
  • Example (TDS Section 194I): Consider the provided scenario for Section 194I with a monthly threshold of ₹50,000 for rent:
    • ABC Ltd. pays ₹75,000 monthly rent to Mr. Sharma (one vendor).
    • Since the monthly rent of ₹75,000 exceeds the ₹50,000 threshold, TDS is applicable on the entire ₹75,000 for that month.
    • The fact that ABC Ltd. might have other landlords (different vendors, potentially with the same PAN as Mr. Sharma for other properties) is irrelevant for this specific vendor and the ₹50,000 monthly threshold for rent under Section 194I. TDS will be calculated on the full rent paid to each landlord individually if their respective rent exceeds the threshold.

 

Additional Information

For further detailed guidance and specific configuration steps within SAP S/4HANA, especially considering whether you are using the cloud or on-premise version, SAP has provided several helpful resources.

Refer to the SAP Notes 3587527 and 3580598 for changes related to TDS sections 194I and 194B in Cloud and On-premise environments, respectively.

Specific configurations for the newly introduced Section 194T on payments by partnership firms to partners are detailed in the SAP Notes 3590606 and 3590334.

Additionally, comprehensive information on configuring TAN Based Exemption can be found in the SAP Help Portal.

For a holistic understanding of withholding tax configurations, both Withholding Tax Configurations - Cloud and Withholding Tax Configuration – On-prem pages on the SAP Help Portal offer valuable insights.

Leveraging these resources will ensure accurate and efficient implementation of the updated TDS regulations in your SAP S/4HANA system.

 

Conclusion

By carefully implementing these configuration steps and adhering to the recommendations, you can effectively align your SAP S/4HANA or SAP ECC system with the updated TDS provisions of the Budget 2025, ensuring smooth tax compliance and accurate financial reporting. Remember to thoroughly test these changes in a non-production environment before implementing them in your live system to ensure a smooth transition and accurate financial and billing processes. Always consult with your SAP consultants for any complex configurations or specific business requirements.
